summary: In an NPR segment discussing the results of a 10-year Women's Health Study, Julie E. Buring comments on the value of negative results in clinical trials. She emphasizes that discovering a treatment doesn't work is crucial because it prevents people from relying on ineffective pills at the expense of proven health interventions.

principles.statement: Knowing what doesn't work is just as important as knowing what does work.
principles.rationale: If people believe an ineffective treatment works, they might rely on it instead of pursuing other interventions that actually provide health benefits.
principles.source_quote: It's very important for us to know what works as well as what doesn't work. Sometimes we make a choice and take something thinking it will benefit us, but we might be doing it instead of something else.

signature_quotes.text: It's very important for us to know what works as well as what doesn't work. Sometimes we make a choice and take something thinking it will benefit us, but we might be doing it instead of something else.
signature_quotes.context: Defending the value of a $40 million study that yielded negative results for vitamin E and aspirin in preventing cancer and heart disease in healthy women.

anti_patterns.description: Taking an unproven pill or supplement under the assumption that it will provide a health benefit.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates an opportunity cost; people might rely on the ineffective pill instead of adopting proven lifestyle changes or treatments.
